English: IMPRECATORY PHRASES
All three wishing evil upon an enemy.

Manx: My lostey lhoam er eh.
English: My bare burning on him.

Manx: Giare ny heill orroo.
English: A short life on them.

Manx: Skeab orroo.
English: The besom [broom] on them — May they be swept out of the way.

Manx: ooriltagh
English: [I will give you] refreshment (I will clout you).

Manx: My hiaght mynney mollaght ort.
English: My seven swearings of a curse on thee.

Note, — The Irish and Scotch representatives of the Manx word mynnye mean swearing, cursing (mionna, mionach), and are used in the Irish and Scotch versions of the Bible. Mollaght mynney — a curse of swearing, a sworn curse. “Loo mollaght mynney ad dy stroie” (Fin as Oshin). The later form is mynney mollaght — swearing of a curse. “My hiaght mynney mollaght ort (Mylecharaine). i.e., “My seven swearings of a curse on thee.”
RHYMES AND STORIES.

Manx: Feeagh doo, feeagh doo, nagh vaik 00 ny bochillyn?
English: Black raven, black raven, did you see the shepherds?

Manx: T’ad ny hoie gob ry ghob cooyl-chlea yn clieau.
English: They are sitting face to face by the mountain hedge.

Manx: Ta mee gollish myr eoylley,
English: I am sweating like dung [manure],

Manx: Whaaley cooat feailley,
English: Stitching the holiday coat,

Manx: Tummey yn snaie ayns ushtey
English: Dipping the needle in water

Manx: Son nagh goghe ee aile ;
English: Lest it take [catch] fire ;

Manx: Myr ta’n snaie cheet jeh bluggan,
English: As the thread comes off a ball,

Manx: T’eh goll ayns snaid lesh whush ayn.
English: It goes into the needle with a whiz in it.
Sung by an old tailor at his work. The last two lines are not always quoted, but I myself heard them sung lately in Peel:

Manx: Ny mraane oast ta’d stoamey,
English: The landladies are smart,

Manx: Tra ta mish shooyl my voght,
English: While I am but a fool,

Manx: As ooilley eiraght my ayraghyn
English: And all my father’s heritage

Manx: Currit ayn son jough.
English: Given away for ale.

Manx: Ny paitchyn ain ta roost as broigh,
English: Our children naked and in filth

Manx: Nyn kishtey neesht as follym,
English: Our cupboards they are bare ;

Manx: Ta mee-hene my voght lesh giu yn yough,
English: Myself a fool with drinking ale,

Manx: As clooidyn fritlagh orrym.
English: And ragged clouts I wear.

Manx: Ta’n Ollick veg fo’n vink,
English: The little Christmas (O.S.) is under the bench,

Manx: Ta’n Ollick vooar cooyl thie,
English: The big Christmas is at the back of the house,

Manx: Eisht lhig dooin goll er gys y phurt ;
English: Then let us go down to the port;

Manx: Son lane bolg dy yough vie.
English: For a bellyful of good ale
(Another version is in Note and Queries. page 17.)
This nursery jingle, which still lingers in Peel—

Manx: Ollick veg ec y vink,
English: [Little Christmas at the bench,]

Manx: Fill the cup and give me drink.
English: Fill the cup and give me drink.

Manx: Ollick vooar ec y thie.
English: Little Christmas at the house.

Manx: Cappan jough as peash dy pie.
English: A cup of ale and a piece of pie.

Manx: Va dooinney jeh sluight Adam cummal ayns boayl dy row
English: There was a man of Adam’s race, He had a certain dwelling-place;

Manx: Cha rieau eh er yn thalloo, niau, ny niurin ;
English: ’Twas not in earth, in heaven, or hell;

Manx: Insh dou c’raad v’eh cummal?
English: Now tell me where that man did dwell ?
Fragment of a nursery rhyme:

Manx: Sheid geay, sheid as gleashey ny strauyn,
English: Blow wind, blow and stir the straws,

Manx: Ben hie dy fuinney as losht ee e laueyn.
English: A woman went to bake and she burnt her hands.

Manx: Ta mee er chlashtyn ny shenn vraane ginsh da ny paitchyn mysh daa feeagh ren meeiteil cheayrt dy row ;  as ren fer jeu briaght jeh’n fer elley “Cre’n niaght ta lhiat?”
English: I have heard the old women this story to the children about two ravens that met once, and one asked the other what news he had.

Manx: Dooyrt eh, “Ta’n bock bane marroo.”
English: “The white horse is dead,” said he.

Manx: “Vel eh roauyr, vel eh roauyr?” dooyrt eh
English: “Is he fat, is he fat?” said the other.

Manx: “Ooilley ayns oorey, ooilley ayns oorey,” eisht v’eh arryssagh dy ren eh ginsh shen da,
English: “All in gravy, all in gravy,” [said he], then, repenting that he had told him,

Manx: “Craueyn loam, craueyn loam,” dooyrt eh.
English: “Bare bones, bare bones,” said he.

Manx: Ta mish er chlashtyn mysh three deiney ghow orroo dy hoilshagh c’red va yn red stroshey ayns yn seihll.
English: I have heard about three men taking on them to show what was the strongest thing in the world.

Manx: Dooyrt yn chied fer, “Dy re feeyn, son dy yinnagh feeyn cur er dooinney yannoo nhee erbee.”
English: The first man said, “It is wine, for wine would make a man do anything.”

Manx: Dooyrt yn nah ’er “Dy re airh dy yinnagh dooinney red erbee son airh.”
English: The second man said, “It is gold, for men would do anything for gold.”

Manx: Dooyrt yn trass fer, “Dy re ben, dy yinnagh dooinney red erbee son graih mraane.”
English: The third man said, “It is woman, for man would do anything for a woman’s love.”

Manx: As va eshyn coontit dy ve kiart.
English: And he was counted to be right.

Manx: Roie, warree, roie, te yn skillin s’deyrey ren shin rieau cosney.
English: Run, granny, run; it’s the dearest shilling that ever you won.

The little boy said this when his granny went for a hare before the hounds (she being a buitch [witch]). She went as a hare and lay in a bush for the sake of the shilling. None of the hunters knew it was granny until the boy began to shout to her to run.”
